(June 26, 1922 – December 9, 2013) was an American actress. She was nominated for three [Academy Awards](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards") for her roles in the films *[Caged](/wiki/Caged_(1950_film) "Caged (1950 film)")* (1950), *[Detective Story](/wiki/Detective_Story_(1951_film) "Detective Story (1951 film)")* (1951), and *[Interrupted Melody](/wiki/Interrupted_Melody "Interrupted Melody")* (1955), the first of which won her the [Volpi Cup for Best Actress](/wiki/Volpi_Cup_for_Best_Actress "Volpi Cup for Best Actress"). She was also known for her roles in the films *[Of Human Bondage](/wiki/Of_Human_Bondage_(1946_film) "Of Human Bondage (1946 film)")* (1946), *[Scaramouche](/wiki/Scaramouche_(1952_film) "Scaramouche (1952 film)")* (1952), *[The Naked Jungle](/wiki/The_Naked_Jungle "The Naked Jungle")* (1954), *[The Man with the Golden Arm](/wiki/The_Man_with_the_Golden_Arm "The Man with the Golden Arm")* (1955), *[A Hole in the Head](/wiki/A_Hole_in_the_Head "A Hole in the Head")* (1959), *[The Sound of Music](/wiki/The_Sound_of_Music_(film) "The Sound of Music (film)")* (1965), and *[The Oscar](/wiki/The_Oscar_(film) "The Oscar (film)")* (1966).

For the historian and medievalist, see [Eleanor Parker (historian)](/wiki/Eleanor_Parker_(historian) "Eleanor Parker (historian)").

**Eleanor Jean Parker** (June 26, 1922 – December 9, 2013) was an American actress. She was nominated for three [Academy Awards](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards") for her roles in the films *[Caged](/wiki/Caged_(1950_film) "Caged (1950 film)")* (1950), *[Detective Story](/wiki/Detective_Story_(1951_film) "Detective Story (1951 film)")* (1951), and *[Interrupted Melody](/wiki/Interrupted_Melody "Interrupted Melody")* (1955), the first of which won her the [Volpi Cup for Best Actress](/wiki/Volpi_Cup_for_Best_Actress "Volpi Cup for Best Actress"). She was also known for her roles in the films *[Of Human Bondage](/wiki/Of_Human_Bondage_(1946_film) "Of Human Bondage (1946 film)")* (1946), *[Scaramouche](/wiki/Scaramouche_(1952_film) "Scaramouche (1952 film)")* (1952), *[The Naked Jungle](/wiki/The_Naked_Jungle "The Naked Jungle")* (1954), *[The Man with the Golden Arm](/wiki/The_Man_with_the_Golden_Arm "The Man with the Golden Arm")* (1955), *[A Hole in the Head](/wiki/A_Hole_in_the_Head "A Hole in the Head")* (1959), *[The Sound of Music](/wiki/The_Sound_of_Music_(film) "The Sound of Music (film)")* (1965), and *[The Oscar](/wiki/The_Oscar_(film) "The Oscar (film)")* (1966).

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eleanor_Parker&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Eleanor Jean Parker was born on June 26, 1922, in [Cedarville, Ohio](/wiki/Cedarville,_Ohio "Cedarville, Ohio"), the daughter of Lola (née Isett) and Lester Day Parker.[[1]](#cite_note-1) She moved with her family to East Cleveland, Ohio, where she attended public schools and graduated from [Shaw High School](/wiki/Shaw_High_School_(Ohio) "Shaw High School (Ohio)"). "Ever since I can remember, all I wanted to do is act", she said. "But I didn't just dream about it. I worked at it."[[2]](#cite_note-hedda-2)

She appeared in a number of school plays. After graduation, she went to Martha's Vineyard to work on her acting. She got a job as a waitress and was offered a screen test by [20th Century Fox](/wiki/20th_Century_Fox "20th Century Fox"), but turned it down. Wanting to focus on films, she moved to California and started appearing at the [Pasadena Playhouse](/wiki/Pasadena_Playhouse "Pasadena Playhouse").[[2]](#cite_note-hedda-2)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eleanor_Parker&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

### Warner Bros

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eleanor_Parker&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Warner Bros")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Eleanor_Parker_pin-up%2C_Yank%2C_The_Army_Weekly_%281943%29.png/250px-Eleanor_Parker_pin-up%2C_Yank%2C_The_Army_Weekly_%281943%29.png)](/wiki/File:Eleanor_Parker_pin-up,_Yank,_The_Army_Weekly_(1943).png)

Parker featured in [WW2](/wiki/WW2 "WW2") publication, *[Yank, the Army Weekly](/wiki/Yank,_the_Army_Weekly "Yank, the Army Weekly")*, (1943)

She was in the audience one night at Pasadena Playhouse when spotted by a Warners Bros talent scout, Irving Kumin. He offered her a test, and she accepted; the studio signed her to a long-term contract in June 1941.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

She was cast that year in the film *[They Died with Their Boots On](/wiki/They_Died_with_Their_Boots_On "They Died with Their Boots On")*, but her scenes were deleted.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) Her actual film debut was as Nurse Ryan in the short film *Soldiers in White* in 1942.

She was given some decent roles in the B films *[Busses Roar](/wiki/Busses_Roar "Busses Roar")* (1942) and *[The Mysterious Doctor](/wiki/The_Mysterious_Doctor "The Mysterious Doctor")* (1943), and she had a small role in *[Mission to Moscow](/wiki/Mission_to_Moscow "Mission to Moscow")* (1943). This performance impressed Warners, so when [Joan Leslie](/wiki/Joan_Leslie "Joan Leslie") was held up on *Rhapsody in Blue*, Parker replaced her in *[Between Two Worlds](/wiki/Between_Two_Worlds_(1944_film) "Between Two Worlds (1944 film)")* (1944), playing the wife of [Paul Henreid](/wiki/Paul_Henreid "Paul Henreid")'s character.

She stayed in supporting roles for *[Crime by Night](/wiki/Crime_by_Night "Crime by Night")* (1944) and *[The Last Ride](/wiki/The_Last_Ride_(1944_film) "The Last Ride (1944 film)")* (1944), then was given the starring role with Dennis Morgan in *[The Very Thought of You](/wiki/The_Very_Thought_of_You_(film) "The Very Thought of You (film)")* (1944), replacing [Ida Lupino](/wiki/Ida_Lupino "Ida Lupino"). She was given a cameo in *[Hollywood Canteen](/wiki/Hollywood_Canteen_(film) "Hollywood Canteen (film)")* (1944). Warners gave her the choice role of Mildred Rogers in a new version of [Somerset Maugham](/wiki/Somerset_Maugham "Somerset Maugham")'s *[Of Human Bondage](/wiki/Of_Human_Bondage_(1946_film) "Of Human Bondage (1946 film)")* (1946). Although director [Edmund Goulding](/wiki/Edmund_Goulding "Edmund Goulding") called Parker one of the five greatest actresses in America,[[6]](#cite_note-6) previews were not favorable, and the film sat on the shelf for two years before being released to an underwhelming reception. However, in 1953, Parker called it her favorite role.[[7]](#cite_note-howard-7)

Parker later said the "big break" of her career was when she was cast with [John Garfield](/wiki/John_Garfield "John Garfield") in *[Pride of the Marines](/wiki/Pride_of_the_Marines "Pride of the Marines")* (1945). "It was a great part, and who wouldn't look good with John Garfield", she later said. "He was absolutely wonderful."[[8]](#cite_note-noir-8) However, two films that followed with [Errol Flynn](/wiki/Errol_Flynn "Errol Flynn"), the romantic comedy *[Never Say Goodbye](/wiki/Never_Say_Goodbye_(1946_film) "Never Say Goodbye (1946 film)")* (1946) and the drama *[Escape Me Never](/wiki/Escape_Me_Never_(1947_film) "Escape Me Never (1947 film)")* (1947), were box-office disappointments.

Parker was suspended twice by Warners for refusing parts in films – in *[Stallion Road](/wiki/Stallion_Road "Stallion Road")* (where she was replaced by [Alexis Smith](/wiki/Alexis_Smith "Alexis Smith")) and *[Love and Learn](/wiki/Love_and_Learn_(1947_film) "Love and Learn (1947 film)")*.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

She made the comedy *[Voice of the Turtle](/wiki/The_Voice_of_the_Turtle_(film) "The Voice of the Turtle (film)")* (1947, aired today under the title *One for the Book*) with [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan") and was in an adaptation of *[The Woman in White](/wiki/The_Woman_in_White_(1948_film) "The Woman in White (1948 film)")* (1948). She refused to appear in *Somewhere in the City* (1948) so Warners suspended her again; [Virginia Mayo](/wiki/Virginia_Mayo "Virginia Mayo") played the role.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Eleanor_Parker_1948.jpg/250px-Eleanor_Parker_1948.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eleanor_Parker_1948.jpg)

Parker in 1948

Parker then had two years off, and during this time, she married and had a baby. She turned down a role in *[The Hasty Heart](/wiki/The_Hasty_Heart "The Hasty Heart")* (1949), which she wanted to do, but it would have meant going to England, and she did not want to leave her baby alone during its first year. "I probably received my salary for only six months during 1947 and 1948, but I can't regret that", she said. "All my life, I wanted a child, and anything that might happen to me professionally on that account would hardly seem a loss."[[11]](#cite_note-los-11)

She returned in *[Chain Lightning](/wiki/Chain_Lightning_(1950_film) "Chain Lightning (1950 film)")* with [Humphrey Bogart](/wiki/Humphrey_Bogart "Humphrey Bogart"). "I've had my fling at roles that have little or no relation to most people's lives", she said in a 1949 interview. "I want to keep away from such assignments, as I can from now on, even though, as some may say, they mean exercising your skill and talent in acting."[[11]](#cite_note-los-11)

Parker heard about *[Caged](/wiki/Caged_(1950_film) "Caged (1950 film)")* (1950), a film Warners was making of a woman in prison, and she lobbied the role. She got it, and won the 1950 [Volpi Cup](/wiki/Volpi_Cup "Volpi Cup") for Best Actress at the [Venice Film Festival](/wiki/Venice_Film_Festival "Venice Film Festival") and was nominated for an Academy Award.[[12]](#cite_note-12) She also had a good role in the melodrama *[Three Secrets](/wiki/Three_Secrets "Three Secrets")* (1950).

In February 1950, Parker left Warner Bros. after having been under contract there for eight years. Parker had understood that she would star in a film called *Safe Harbor*, but Warner Bros. apparently had no intention of making it. Because of this misunderstanding, her agents negotiated her release.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### Paramount

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eleanor_Parker&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Paramount")]

Parker's career outside of Warners started badly with *[Valentino](/wiki/Valentino_(1951_film) "Valentino (1951 film)")* (1951), where she played a fictionalized wife of [Rudolph Valentino](/wiki/Rudolph_Valentino "Rudolph Valentino"), and then she tried the comedy *[A Millionaire for Christy](/wiki/A_Millionaire_for_Christy "A Millionaire for Christy")* (1951) (originally called *The Golden Goose*).

In 1951, Parker signed a contract with Paramount for one film per year, with an option for outside films.[[14]](#cite_note-14) This arrangement began brilliantly with *[Detective Story](/wiki/Detective_Story_(1951_film) "Detective Story (1951 film)")* (1951) for director [William Wyler](/wiki/William_Wyler "William Wyler"), playing Mary McLeod, the woman who doesn't understand the position of her unstable detective husband (played by [Kirk Douglas](/wiki/Kirk_Douglas "Kirk Douglas")); Parker was nominated for the leading actress [Oscar](/wiki/Academy_Award "Academy Award") in 1951 for her performance, which, to date, remains the shortest performance to be nominated in the category.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

### MGM

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eleanor_Parker&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: MGM")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Scaramouche_%281952%29_trailer_4.jpg/250px-Scaramouche_%281952%29_trailer_4.jpg)](/wiki/File:Scaramouche_(1952)_trailer_4.jpg)

Parker with [Stewart Granger](/wiki/Stewart_Granger "Stewart Granger") in *[Scaramouche](/wiki/Scaramouche_(1952_film) "Scaramouche (1952 film)")* (1952)

Parker followed *Detective Story* with her portrayal of an actress in love with a swashbuckling nobleman (played by [Stewart Granger](/wiki/Stewart_Granger "Stewart Granger")) in *[Scaramouche](/wiki/Scaramouche_(1952_film) "Scaramouche (1952 film)")* (1952), a role originally intended for [Ava Gardner](/wiki/Ava_Gardner "Ava Gardner"). Parker later claimed that Granger was the only person she didn't get along with during her entire career.[[8]](#cite_note-noir-8) However, they had good chemistry, and the film was a massive hit. MGM rushed her into *[Above and Beyond](/wiki/Above_and_Beyond_(1952_film) "Above and Beyond (1952 film)")* (1952), a biopic of Lt. Col. [Paul W. Tibbets, Jr.](/wiki/Paul_Tibbets "Paul Tibbets") (Robert Taylor), the pilot of the aircraft that dropped the atomic bomb on Hiroshima. It was a solid hit. While Parker was making *[Escape from Fort Bravo](/wiki/Escape_from_Fort_Bravo "Escape from Fort Bravo")* (1953), she signed a five-year contract with MGM.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

She was named as star of *My Most Intimate Friend*[[17]](#cite_note-17) and of *One More Time*, from a script by [Ruth Gordon](/wiki/Ruth_Gordon "Ruth Gordon") and [Garson Kanin](/wiki/Garson_Kanin "Garson Kanin"), but neither film was made. Back at Paramount, Parker starred with [Charlton Heston](/wiki/Charlton_Heston "Charlton Heston") as a 1901 mail-order bride in *[The Naked Jungle](/wiki/The_Naked_Jungle "The Naked Jungle")* (1954), directed by [Byron Haskin](/wiki/Byron_Haskin "Byron Haskin") and produced by [George Pal](/wiki/George_Pal "George Pal").

Parker returned to MGM where she was reunited with Robert Taylor in *[Valley of the Kings](/wiki/Valley_of_the_Kings_(film) "Valley of the Kings (film)")* (1954) and the Western *[Many Rivers to Cross](/wiki/Many_Rivers_to_Cross_(film) "Many Rivers to Cross (film)")* (1955).

"I maintain that if you work, believe in yourself, and do what is right for you without stepping all over others, the way somehow opens up", she said in 1953. "By that, I don't mean just sitting back. At Warners, they still have a mile-long list of my suspensions for refusing certain parts. Anyway, I never did a Western. Not once. It's paid off too."[[7]](#cite_note-howard-7)

In a 1954 interview, she said her favorite films were *Caged* and *Detective Story* and her least favorite were *Chain Lightning*, *Escape Me Never*, *Valentino*, and *The Woman in White*. She had commitments to make two films per year at MGM and one per year at Paramount. "Personally, I prefer to be under contract", she said.[[18]](#cite_note-times-18)

MGM gave her one of her better roles as opera singer [Marjorie Lawrence](/wiki/Marjorie_Lawrence "Marjorie Lawrence") in *[Interrupted Melody](/wiki/Interrupted_Melody "Interrupted Melody")* (1955). It was a big hit and earned Parker a third Oscar nomination; she later said it was her favorite film.[[8]](#cite_note-noir-8)

Also in 1955, Parker appeared in the film adaptation of the [National Book Award](/wiki/National_Book_Award "National Book Award")-winner *[The Man with the Golden Arm](/wiki/The_Man_with_the_Golden_Arm "The Man with the Golden Arm")* (1955), directed by [Otto Preminger](/wiki/Otto_Preminger "Otto Preminger") and released through [United Artists](/wiki/United_Artists "United Artists"). She played Zosh, a woman in a wheelchair and the wife of heroin-addicted would-be jazz drummer Frankie Machine ([Frank Sinatra](/wiki/Frank_Sinatra "Frank Sinatra")). It was a major commercial and critical success.

In 1956, she was billed above the title with [Clark Gable](/wiki/Clark_Gable "Clark Gable") for the comedy *[The King and Four Queens](/wiki/The_King_and_Four_Queens "The King and Four Queens")*, also for United Artists.

It was then back to MGM for two movies, both dramas: *[Lizzie](/wiki/Lizzie_(1957_film) "Lizzie (1957 film)")* (1957), in the title role, as a woman with a split personality, and *[The Seventh Sin](/wiki/The_Seventh_Sin "The Seventh Sin")* (1957), a remake of *[The Painted Veil](/wiki/The_Painted_Veil_(1934_film) "The Painted Veil (1934 film)")*. Both films flopped at the box office, and as a result, Parker's plans to produce *L'Eternelle*, about French resistance fighters, did not materialize.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### Later films, and transition into television and theatre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eleanor_Parker&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Later films, and transition into television and theatre")]

Parker supported [Frank Sinatra](/wiki/Frank_Sinatra "Frank Sinatra") in the popular comedy *[A Hole in the Head](/wiki/A_Hole_in_the_Head "A Hole in the Head")* (1959). She returned to MGM for *[Home from the Hill](/wiki/Home_from_the_Hill_(film) "Home from the Hill (film)")* (1960), co-starring with [Robert Mitchum](/wiki/Robert_Mitchum "Robert Mitchum"), then took over [Lana Turner](/wiki/Lana_Turner "Lana Turner")'s role of Constance Rossi in *[Return to Peyton Place](/wiki/Return_to_Peyton_Place_(film) "Return to Peyton Place (film)")*, a 1961 sequel to the hit 1957 film. It was made by 20th Century Fox which produced *[Madison Avenue](/wiki/Madison_Avenue_(film) "Madison Avenue (film)")* (1961) with Parker.

In 1960, she made her TV debut. "I look for the quality story and for parts that I think will be good or fun. People told me I was crazy to do *Hole in the Head* and *Home from the Hill*, but both those pictures appealed to me. I did enough of the bad ones (films), while I was under contract – because I was being told to do them. That's the problem with being under contract. You do the pictures, or be suspended. Now, I don't want to work unless I have faith in the part. This has nothing to do with wanting to be famous, or anything like that. It's just that I love acting."[[20]](#cite_note-20)

In the early 1960s, she worked increasingly in television, with the occasional film role such as *[Panic Button](/wiki/Panic_Button_(1964_film) "Panic Button (1964 film)")* (1964).

Parker's best-known screen role was playing Baroness Elsa von Schraeder in the 1965 [Oscar](/wiki/Academy_Award "Academy Award")-winning musical *[The Sound of Music](/wiki/The_Sound_of_Music_(film) "The Sound of Music (film)")*.

In 1966, she played an alcoholic widow in the crime drama *[Warning Shot](/wiki/Warning_Shot_(1967_film) "Warning Shot (1967 film)")*, a talent scout who discovers a Hollywood star in *[The Oscar](/wiki/The_Oscar_(film) "The Oscar (film)")*, and a rich alcoholic in *[An American Dream](/wiki/An_American_Dream_(film) "An American Dream (film)")*. From the late 1960s, she focused on television roles.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Eleanor_Park_star_HWF.JPG/250px-Eleanor_Park_star_HWF.JPG)](/wiki/File:Eleanor_Park_star_HWF.JPG)

Star on the [Hollywood Walk of Fame](/wiki/Hollywood_Walk_of_Fame "Hollywood Walk of Fame") at 6340 Hollywood Blvd.

In 1963, Parker appeared in the medical drama *[The Eleventh Hour](/wiki/The_Eleventh_Hour_(1962_TV_series) "The Eleventh Hour (1962 TV series)")* in the episode "Why Am I Grown So Cold?", for which she was nominated for an [Emmy Award](/wiki/Emmy_Award "Emmy Award") as Outstanding Single Performance by an Actress in a Leading Role. In 1964, she appeared in the episode "A Land More Cruel" on *[Breaking Point](/wiki/Breaking_Point_(1963_TV_series) "Breaking Point (1963 TV series)")*. In 1968, she portrayed a spy in *[How to Steal the World](/wiki/How_to_Steal_the_World "How to Steal the World")*, a film originally shown as the two-part concluding episodes of *[The Man from U.N.C.L.E.](/wiki/The_Man_from_U.N.C.L.E. "The Man from U.N.C.L.E.")*.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Parker starred with Michael Sarrazin and Gayle Hunnicutt in her final theatrical film of the 1960s, the tense thriller *Eye of The Cat* (1969), which was written by [Joseph Stefano](/wiki/Joseph_Stefano "Joseph Stefano").

In 1969–1970, Parker starred in the television series *[Bracken's World](/wiki/Bracken%27s_World "Bracken's World")*, for which she was nominated for a 1970 [Golden Globe Award](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award "Golden Globe Award") as Best TV Actress – Drama. "I wanted to do the series so I could stay put", she said. "Every movie I'm offered is shot in Europe or Asia or somewhere. I'm tired of running around."[[22]](#cite_note-22) Parker left the series after the first 16 episodes, citing the limited nature of her role.

After 1969, she worked steadily, but except for a small role in *[Sunburn](/wiki/Sunburn_(1979_film) "Sunburn (1979 film)")* (1979), her onscreen acting was on television. Parker appeared in the NBC series *[Ghost Story](/wiki/Ghost_Story_(TV_series) "Ghost Story (TV series)")* episode "Half a Death" (1972). Parker appeared in the TV movie *Maybe I'll Come Home in the Spring* (1971) and on TV in *Home for the Holidays* (1972). She starred in other TV movies and made guest appearances on series such as *[Hawaii Five-O](/wiki/Hawaii_Five-O_(1968_TV_series) "Hawaii Five-O (1968 TV series)")*, *[The Love Boat](/wiki/The_Love_Boat "The Love Boat")*, *[Hotel](/wiki/Hotel_(U.S._TV_series) "Hotel (U.S. TV series)")*, and *[Murder, She Wrote](/wiki/Murder,_She_Wrote "Murder, She Wrote")*. Her final TV role was in the 1991 TV movie *Dead on the Money*.

Concurrent with her TV career, Parker starred in a number of theatrical productions, including the role of Margo Channing in *[Applause](/wiki/Applause_(musical) "Applause (musical)")*, the Broadway musical version of the film *[All About Eve](/wiki/All_About_Eve "All About Eve")*. The role originally was played in the musical by [Lauren Bacall](/wiki/Lauren_Bacall "Lauren Bacall"). In 1976, she played Maxine in the [Ahmanson Theatre](/wiki/Ahmanson_Theatre "Ahmanson Theatre") revival of *[The Night of the Iguana](/wiki/The_Night_of_the_Iguana "The Night of the Iguana")*. She was replaced in the [Circle in the Square Theatre](/wiki/Circle_in_the_Square_Theatre "Circle in the Square Theatre") revival of *[Pal Joey](/wiki/Pal_Joey_(musical) "Pal Joey (musical)")* during previews.[[23]](#cite_note-IBDB-23)

For her contributions to the movie industry, Parker was honored with a star on the [Hollywood Walk of Fame](/wiki/Hollywood_Walk_of_Fame "Hollywood Walk of Fame") at 6340 Hollywood Boulevard.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eleanor_Parker&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Parker was married four times:

* Fred Losee – married in April 1943, divorced in December 1944.[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)
* Bert E. Friedlob – married in 1946, divorced in 1953; the marriage produced three children.[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)
* Paul Clemens, American portrait painter – married in 1954, divorced in 1965; the marriage produced one child, actor Paul Clemens.[[29]](#cite_note-29)
* Raymond N. Hirsch – married in 1966, widowed on September 14, 2001, when Hirsch died of esophageal cancer.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

She was the grandmother of actor [Chasen Parker](/wiki/Chasen_Parker "Chasen Parker").[[31]](#cite_note-31)

Parker was raised a Protestant, and later converted to [Messianic Judaism](/wiki/Messianic_Judaism "Messianic Judaism"), a form of Christianity which takes on some Jewish characteristics, telling the *New York Daily News* columnist [Kay Gardella](/wiki/Kay_Gardella "Kay Gardella") in August 1969: "I think we're all Jews at heart...I wanted to convert for a long time." She embraced and was a supporter of Messianic Jewish philosopher, teacher, and commentator [Roy Masters](/wiki/Roy_Masters_(commentator) "Roy Masters (commentator)"), owner of the [Foundation of Human Understanding](/wiki/Foundation_of_Human_Understanding "Foundation of Human Understanding") in Grants Pass, Oregon. In 1978, she wrote the foreword to Masters's book *How Your Mind Can Keep You Well*.,[[32]](#cite_note-32)

## Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eleanor_Parker&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Death")]

Eleanor Parker died on December 9, 2013, at a medical facility in Palm Springs, California, from complications of pneumonia. She was 91.[[33]](#cite_note-33)
